join_order.note: probe is parallel sequential scan; 50K-entry hash set fits entirely in L3 (44MB)
build once on customer, probe once per qualifying order; trivial at 50K probes

filter_pushdown.predicate: GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM(l_quantity) > 30000 (raw scaled value)
filter_pushdown.note: 15M unique l_orderkey groups collapse to ~50K qualifying entries
semi-join eliminates ~99.7% of orders rows before any join

subquery_reuse_optimization.description: Phase 1 aggregation (l_orderkey -> sum_qty_raw) produces both the qualifying set (HAVING filter) and the outer query's SUM(l_quantity). No second lineitem scan required.

data_structures.phase1_thread_local_agg: compact_open_addressing_hash_map_per_thread(l_orderkey -> int64_t sum_qty_raw, initial_capacity=1M per thread, multiply_shift_hash)
data_structures.phase1_global_merged: partitioned_hash_map(16 partitions, ~15M unique groups total, merged from 64 thread-locals)
data_structures.phase1_qualifying_set: compact_flat_hash_map(int32_t orderkey -> int64_t sum_qty_raw, ~50K entries, reserve(65536), fits in L3 cache)
data_structures.customer_hash: flat_hash_map(int32_t c_custkey -> uint32_t row_index, pre_sized 1.5M, load_factor=0.5, multiply_shift_hash)
data_structures.result_buffer: flat SOA array of ~50K tuples: {c_name_str, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, sum_qty_raw}

phase1_lineitem_scan.pattern: each thread maintains thread_local compact_hash_map; after parallel scan, merge 64 thread-locals into 16-partition global map
phase1_lineitem_scan.having_filter_applied: after merge, iterate global map and collect entries with sum_qty_raw > 30000

phase2_orders_scan.pattern: parallel scan of 15M orders; each thread probes shared (read-only) phase1_qualifying_set; thread-local result buffers; merge after scan
phase2_orders_scan.note: qualifying_set is read-only after Phase 1 — no synchronization needed during probe

parallelism.phase2_customer_lookup: serial — only 50K lookups after parallel orders scan

index_rationale.l_orderkey_hash.idx: NOT used — Phase 1 performs a single full sequential scan of lineitem (60M rows) for aggregation; random index lookups on HDD would be slower and the sequential scan is unavoidable
index_rationale.orders_o_orderkey_hash.idx: NOT used — scanning all 15M orders sequentially and probing the 50K-entry hash set is faster than 15M random index lookups
index_rationale.orders_o_custkey_hash.idx: NOT used — customer hash map (1.5M entries built once) probed for 50K orders is sufficient; index would add overhead
index_rationale.customer_c_custkey_hash.idx: NOT used — building a fresh flat_hash_map on customer during Phase 2 setup is faster than loading and using a file-based index for 50K lookups

late_materialization.materialization_point: after phase2_customer_lookup — load c_name string only for ~50K qualifying rows by row index
late_materialization.reason: avoids loading 1.5M variable-length strings; load only ~50K strings (3.3% of table)

phase1_subquery_aggregation.description: Parallel scan all 60M lineitem rows; aggregate SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey; filter HAVING sum_qty_raw > 30000; store qualifying (orderkey, sum_qty_raw) pairs

phase2_main_join.description: Parallel scan 15M orders, semi-join with phase1 qualifying set; lookup customer for each qualifying order; assemble result tuples

step1_build_customer_hash.note: c_name loaded later via late materialization

step3_customer_lookup.description: For each qualifying order, probe customer_hash_map with o_custkey to get row_index; load c_name via late materialization
step3_customer_lookup.output: complete result tuples with c_name, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, sum_qty_raw

step4_aggregation.description: GROUP BY (c_name, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice) — trivially one row per qualifying order since o_orderkey is unique; sum_qty already computed in Phase 1
step4_aggregation.strategy: identity — no actual grouping needed since o_orderkey is PK of orders

phase3_sort_topk.description: Sort ~50K result rows by (o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC), emit top 100
phase3_sort_topk.complexity: O(N log K) = O(50000 * log(100)) ≈ trivial
phase3_sort_topk.sort_key: composite: negate o_totalprice for DESC, o_orderdate for ASC
phase3_sort_topk.note: 50K rows easily fits in L3 cache; serial partial_sort is optimal

phase_breakdown.phase1_lineitem_scan_parallel: ~80-100ms (60M rows / 64 threads, HDD sequential if cold; L3-cached if warm)
